title: John draws down upon Himself the Displeasure of Many Persons of Rank and Power. Of the Eunuch Eutropius.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As long as John was in conflict with the clergy only, machinations against him were utterly powerless; but when he proceeded to rebuke many of those in public office also with immoderate vehemence, the tide of unpopularity began to set against him with far greater impetus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Hence many stories were told to his disparagement.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And most of these found attentive and believing listeners.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This growing prejudice was not a little increased by an oration which he pronounced at that time against Eutropius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For Eutropius was the chief eunuch of the imperial bed-chamber, and the first of all eunuchs that was admitted to the dignity of consul.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He, desiring to inflict vengeance on certain persons who had taken refuge in the churches, induced the emperors to make a law excluding delinquents from the privilege of sanctuary, and authorizing the seizure of those who had sought the shelter of the sacred edifices.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But its author was punished for this almost immediately; for scarcely had the law been promulgated, before Eutropius himself, having incurred the displeasure of the emperor, fled for protection to the church.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The bishop therefore, while Eutropius trembling with fear lay under the table of the altar, mounting the pulpit from which he was accustomed to address the people in order to be the more distinctly heard, uttered an invective against him: wherefore he seemed to create greater displeasure in some, as he not only denied compassion to the unfortunate, but added insult to cruelty.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: By the emperor’s order however, for certain offences committed by him, Eutropius, though bearing the consulate, was decapitated, and his name effaced from the list of consuls, that of Theodore his colleague being alone suffered to remain as in office for that year.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It is said that John afterwards used the same license towards Gaïnas also, who was then commander-in-chief of the army; treating him with characteristic rudeness, because he had presumed to request the emperor to assign the Arians, with whom he agreed in sentiment, one of the churches within the city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Many others also of the higher orders, for a variety of causes, he censured with the same unceremonious freedom, so that by these means he created many powerful adversaries.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Wherefore Theophilus bishop of Alexandria, immediately after his ordination, was plotting his overthrow; and concerted measures for this purpose in secret, both with the friends who were around him, and by letter with such as were at a distance.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For it was not so much the boldness with which John lashed whatever was obnoxious to him, that affected Theophilus, as his own failure to place his favorite presbyter Isidore in the episcopal chair of Constantinople.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In such a state were the affairs of John the bishop at that time; mischief thus threatened him at the very commencement of his episcopate.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But we shall enter into these things more at large as we proceed.
